### shared/filesystem-conventions
---
name: filesystem-conventions
description: How the factory workspace is organized and what each directory means.
---

## Workspace Structure

```
factory/
├── agents.yaml          # Agent definitions — only the human edits this
├── specs/
│   ├── inbox/           # Raw intents from the human
│   ├── drafting/        # Spec agent is working on these
│   ├── ready/           # Specced and ready for Builder
│   └── archive/         # Completed specs (reference library)
├── tasks/
│   ├── research/        # Research requests from any agent → Researcher picks up
│   ├── research-done/   # Completed research briefs → requesting agent reads
│   ├── building/        # Builder's work-in-progress (Verifier CANNOT see)
│   ├── review/          # Completed work awaiting verification
│   ├── verified/        # Passed verification
│   ├── failed/          # Failed verification (with failure reports)
│   └── maintenance/     # Ongoing ops tasks (Operator's domain)
├── scenarios/           # HOLDOUT — Builder cannot read this
├── skills/
│   ├── shared/          # Approved skills for all agents
│   ├── proposed/        # Self-authored skills awaiting Librarian review
│   └── {agent}/         # Agent-specific skills
├── memory/
│   ├── shared/          # Cross-agent knowledge (Librarian curates)
│   │   ├── KNOWLEDGE.md # Durable facts, decisions, conventions
│   │   └── PROJECTS.md  # Master project status
│   ├── daily/{agent}/   # Append-only daily logs
│   └── {agent}/MEMORY.md # Private curated memory
├── learnings/
│   ├── failures/        # What went wrong and why
│   ├── corrections/     # Human corrections (highest signal)
│   └── discoveries/     # New patterns, better approaches
├── universe/            # Reference docs — read-only for all agents
├── projects/            # The actual codebases (git repos)
└── runs/                # Execution history
```

## Cardinal Rules

The filesystem IS the coordination protocol. Don't try to "message" other agents. Write files where other agents will find them on their next turn.

Moving a file between directories IS a state transition:
- `specs/inbox/` → `specs/drafting/` = "Spec is working on this"
- `specs/drafting/` → `specs/ready/` = "Spec is done, ready for Builder"
- `tasks/building/` → `tasks/review/` = "Builder is done, verify this"
- `tasks/review/` → `tasks/verified/` = "Verifier approves"
- `tasks/review/` → `tasks/failed/` = "Verifier rejects with report"

Git tracks everything. Commit after every meaningful state change.

## File Naming

- Learnings: `{YYYY-MM-DD}-{project}-{brief-summary}.md`
- Daily logs: `memory/daily/{agent}/{YYYY-MM-DD}.md`
- Research requests: `tasks/research/{requesting-agent}-{brief-question}.md`
- Research briefs: `tasks/research-done/{request-id}.md`
- Skills: `skills/{scope}/{skill-name}/SKILL.md`
- Subagent tasks: `tasks/{parent-agent}-sub/{sub-id}.yaml`
- Subagent output: `tasks/{parent-agent}-sub/{sub-id}/output.md`

## The `universe/` Directory

This contains the reference documents the factory was designed from — Attractor specs, context engineering principles, our own synthesis. Any agent can read it. No agent can write to it. Use it to check your work against the source thinking.


### shared/context-discipline
---
name: context-discipline
description: How to manage your context window. Load only what you need. Write to disk before you forget.
---

## The Context Budget

Your context window is a finite resource. It degrades predictably as it fills:

- GREEN (0-30%): Sharp attention. Full fidelity. Stay here for critical work.
- YELLOW (30-50%): Still good. Lost-in-the-middle effect begins. Middle content gets less attention.
- ORANGE (50-70%): Active degradation. Instructions compete with content volume. Load only what you need.
- RED (70-90%): Diminishing returns. Each new token steals attention from existing tokens.
- DARK RED (90%+): Failure territory. Write everything to disk immediately.

## Before Starting Any Task

1. Read only the specific spec/task you're working on
2. Load only the skills relevant to THIS task — not every skill you have access to
3. Read your private MEMORY.md for relevant prior context
4. Read shared KNOWLEDGE.md only if the task involves cross-project concerns
5. Check your context level. If you're already in YELLOW from setup, you've loaded too much.

## During Long Tasks

If you've been working for many turns, pause and write a checkpoint to your daily log:
- Your current understanding of the task
- What you've done so far
- What remains
- Any decisions you've made and why

This protects you from context compaction losing important state.

## When Finishing a Task

- Write a summary to your daily log
- If you learned anything durable, write to `learnings/`
- Don't carry context from one task to the next — start fresh

## The Critical Rule

When the runtime warns you about context level, STOP what you're doing and write state to disk. Don't try to "finish just this one thing." The quality of your output is already degrading. Persist, exit, restart clean.


### shared/self-improvement
---
name: self-improvement
description: When you encounter a failure, correction, or new pattern, write a learning and optionally propose a skill.
---

## When to Activate

- A tool call fails unexpectedly
- The human corrects you
- You discover a better approach to something you've done before
- You notice a pattern across multiple tasks
- An external tool or API behaves differently than expected

## What to Do

1. Write a learning to the appropriate directory:
   - `learnings/failures/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{project}-{summary}.md` for failures
   - `learnings/corrections/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{summary}.md` for human corrections
   - `learnings/discoveries/{YYYY-MM-DD}-{summary}.md` for new patterns

   Include: what happened, what you expected, what actually occurred, what you learned, what should change.

2. If the learning suggests a reusable skill (something that would help ANY agent in similar future situations):
   - Write a SKILL.md to `skills/proposed/{skill-name}/SKILL.md`
   - Librarian will review it on next heartbeat
   - Keep it concise — under 200 lines. If it's longer, it's trying to do too much.

3. Update your daily log with a brief note about the learning.

## What NOT to Do

- Don't modify skills in `skills/shared/` directly — always propose via `skills/proposed/`
- Don't write a skill for something that happened once. Wait for patterns (2-3 occurrences).
- Don't write learnings that are just "this didn't work." Always include analysis: WHY it didn't work and what to do differently.
- Don't write a skill that duplicates an existing one. Check `skills/shared/` first.


### shared/decomposition
---
name: decomposition
description: When and how to spawn subagents for parallel work. Any root agent can decompose.
---

## When to Decompose

- A task has 3+ independent facets that could be explored in parallel
- You're about to do the same thing serially for multiple items
- The task would push your context past YELLOW (>30%) if done in a single session
- You need to try multiple approaches and pick the best one

## When NOT to Decompose

- The task is sequential (step 2 depends on step 1's output)
- The overhead of defining subagents exceeds the work itself
- The task fits comfortably in GREEN zone as a single effort
- You're already a subagent (no recursive decomposition — max depth = 1)

## How to Decompose

1. Write a brief decomposition plan to your daily log
2. For each subagent, write a task file to `tasks/{your-name}-sub/`:
   ```yaml
   objective: "One paragraph — what this subagent should accomplish"
   skills: [list of skill paths to load]
   read: [list of directories/files to give access to]
   write: tasks/{your-name}-sub/{sub-id}/output.md
   model: claude-haiku-4-5  # subagents use cheap, fast models
   max_context_fraction: 0.30  # hard cap at GREEN zone
   ```
3. The runtime spawns the subagents (potentially in parallel)
4. When subagents complete, read their outputs
5. SYNTHESIZE — don't just concatenate. Your value is in the synthesis.
6. Subagent files are ephemeral. Librarian may harvest learnings but the rest is cleaned up.

## Subagent Constraints

- Inherit specified skills but NOT your memory (they start clean)
- Cannot spawn their own subagents
- Cannot write to `skills/proposed/` — only root agents propose skills
- Write ONLY to their designated output path
- Hard-capped at GREEN zone context budget


## Available Skills
The following skills are available to you. To activate a skill, request it by name and I will load its full content.

- knowledge-curation: How to review proposed skills, curate shared memory, and detect patterns.

     6→## Reviewing Proposed Skills
     7→
     8→When you find new files in `skills/proposed/`:
     9→
    10→1. Read the proposed SKILL.md

    13→4. Check: Does it conflict with existing skills in `skills/shared/`?
    14→5. Check: Is it well-written? Could another agent follow it without confusion?
    15→
    16→If it passes: move to `skills/shared/`, commit with a note about what was reviewed.
    17→If it needs work: edit it in `skills/proposed/`, leave a note about what changed.
    18→If it's redundant or wrong: delete it, write a brief explanation in `learnings/`.
    19→
    20→## Pattern Detection
    21→
    22→Scan `learnings/failures/` for recurring themes. If Builder hits the same class of problem 3+ times, that's a pattern. Write a skill for it.
    23→
    24→Scan daily logs across agents for repeated workarounds. If agents keep doing the same manual step, that's a skill waiting to be written.
    25→
    26→## Memory Hygiene
    27→
    28→- Daily logs older than 7 days: scan for anything worth promoting to MEMORY.md or KNOWLEDGE.md, then archive
    29→- KNOWLEDGE.md: check for contradictions, outdated facts, redundancies
    30→- PROJECTS.md: update project statuses based on recent task movements

{"type": "user", "message": {"role": "user", "content": [{"tool_use_id": "toolu_01ChbkBVQ1rkt91wLZfHspE3", "type": "tool_result", "content": "     1→---
     2→name: source-evaluation
     3→description: How to judge whether information is reliable. Applies a source hierarchy before incorporating any finding into a research brief.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Source Hierarchy
     7→
     8→When evaluating information, apply this ordering — prefer higher tiers:
     9→
    10→1. **Official documentation** — vendor docs, RFC specs, language references
    11→2. **Primary source code** — the actual library/project, not descriptions of it
    12→3. **Recent authoritative blog posts** — from known maintainers or recognized experts
    13→4. **Stack Overflow / community Q&A** — useful for patterns, check answer date and vote count
    14→5. **LLM training data recall** — treat as a starting point only, not ground truth
    15→
    16→When two sources conflict, the higher-tier source wins. When a lower-tier source
    17→contradicts official docs, note the discrepancy rather than silently resolving it.
    18→
    19→## Recency Check
    20→
    21→- API and library guidance older than 2 years is suspect — check for version drift
    22→- Security guidance must be current — old advisories may describe fixed issues
    23→- Architectural patterns are longer-lived — age alone does not disqualify them
    24→- Always note the date or version of a key source in the research brief
    25→
    26→## Maintenance Status
    27→
    28→Before recommending a library or tool, check:
    29→- Last release date and commit frequency
    30→- Open issues vs. closed issues ratio
    31→- Whether the README references abandonment or a maintained fork
    32\u219

{"type": "user", "message": {"role": "user", "content": [{"tool_use_id": "toolu_01DHEsGR74RQ58DebrZZP5DQ", "type": "tool_result", "content": "     1→---
     2→name: recommendation-format
     3→description: How to deliver research findings as a decision-ready brief. Not a research paper — an actionable answer with trade-offs surfaced.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## The Brief Structure
     7→
     8→Every research output written to `tasks/research-done/` must follow this structure:
     9→
    10→```
    11→## Question
    12→{Restate the question you were asked to answer — one sentence}
    13→
    14→## Options Considered
    15→{List the options evaluated, even those you ruled out quickly}
    16→
    17→## Recommendation
    18→{One clear recommendation — not "it depends" without a resolution}
    19→
    20→## Reasoning
    21→{Why this option over the others — specific, not generic}
    22→
    23→## Trade-offs
    24→{What you lose by choosing this recommendation over the alternatives}
    25→
    26→## Confidence
    27→{High / Medium / Low — and why}
    28→
    29→## Sources
    30→{Key sources with dates or versions}
    31→```
    32→
    33→**Trade-offs** — The most important section for avoiding surprise. A recommendation
    34→without trade-offs is an incomplete recommendation.
    35→
    36→**Confidence** — Honest calibration. Low confidence on a minor question is fine. Low
    37→confidence on a core architectural choice is a flag to request deeper research or human
    38→input.
    39→
    40→## Length
    41→
    42→Stay under 500 words. If you need more, the question is too broad — narrow it first.
    43→

    18→
    19→**Artifact-by-Artifact Assessment** — For each deliverable the spec describes:
    20→- What the spec requires (quote it if brief)

    26→**Path to Resolution** — Concrete steps Builder would need to take to bring the

    29→**Generalizable Learning** — The class of failure, why it occurred, what future
    30→specs or implementations should do differently. See `verification-protocol` skill.

     5→
     6→## CLI Tool
     7→
     8→```
     9→## Overview
    10→{What it does in one sentence}
    11→
    12→## Behavioral Requirements
    13→- Accepts {arguments/flags}
    14→- Outputs {format} to {stdout/file}
    15→- Handles {error cases} with {behavior}
    16→
    17→## Interface Boundaries
    18→### CLI interface
    19→{Exact command syntax and flag descriptions}
    20→### Output format
    21→{Schema or example output}
    22→
    23→## Constraints
    24→- No external dependencies beyond stdlib (or: depends on {libraries})
    25→- Must run on Python 3.10+
    26→
    27→## Verification Criteria
    28→- {Concrete, testable condition}
    29→- {Concrete, testable condition}
    30→```
    31→
    32→The same structure — Overview, Behavioral Requirements, Interface Boundaries, Constraints,
    33→Verification Criteria — applies to all project types (Web APIs, data pipelines, libraries).
    34→Adapt section contents to fit the project type's conventions and vocabulary.
    35→
    36→## Filling in the Skeleton
    37→
    38→- Every spec must have Verification Criteria that are testable without human judgment
    39→- Behavioral Requirements describe what the system does, not how it does it
    40→- Interface Boundaries are contracts — be precise about schemas and types
    41→- Constraints are hard limits — if something is merely a preference, say so

{"type": "user", "message": {"role": "user", "content": [{"tool_use_id": "toolu_01K3wbdeu61zDZ8yThdz8XHc", "type": "tool_result", "content": "     1→---
     2→name: satisfaction-scoring
     3→description: The probabilistic satisfaction metric. Not pass/fail — a judgment about the fraction of users who would be satisfied with this output.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## The Metric
     7→
     8→The satisfaction score answers this question:
     9→
    10→> "Of a population of users who wanted what the spec describes, what fraction
    11→> would be satisfied with this output?"
    12→
    13→Express as N/10. This is a judgment about fitness for purpose, calibrated against
    14→the spec's stated intent — not a code quality score, completeness checklist, or
    15→binary pass/fail.
    16→
    17→## Scoring Guidelines
    18→
    19→**9-10/10** — All verification criteria satisfied, no unexpected behaviors.
    20→**7-8/10** — Core behavior correct. Minor gaps most users wouldn't encounter.
    21→**5-6/10** — Core behavior partially correct. Missing behaviors a meaningful fraction needs.
    22→**3-4/10** — Key requirements not met.
    23→**1-2/10** — Fundamental misalignment with the spec.
    24→
    25→## Required Reasoning
    26→
    27→Always accompany the score with:
    28→- Which criteria were fully satisfied
    29→- Which criteria were partially satisfied or missed
    30→- Why you placed the score where you did rather than one point higher or lower
    31→
    32→## Threshold
    33→
    34→A score of 8/10 or higher moves to `tasks/verified/`.
    35→A score of 7/10 or below moves to `tasks/failed/` with a full failure report.

{"type": "user", "message": {"role": "user", "content": [{"tool_use_id": "toolu_01UU8khRox4iy8Xx7Tfps6qn", "type": "tool_result", "content": "     1→---
     2→name: pattern-detection
     3→description: How to spot recurring patterns across projects. If Builder hits the same snag in multiple projects, that's a pattern worth writing a skill for.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## What Counts as a Pattern
     7→
     8→A pattern is a recurring situation — not a one-off event. Before writing a skill,
     9→confirm that the pattern has appeared in at least 2-3 independent contexts.
    10→
    11→Signals that something is a pattern:
    12→- The same class of failure appears in `learnings/failures/` more than once
    13→- Builder's daily logs mention the same difficulty across different projects
    14→- Multiple proposed skills address the same underlying situation
    15→
    16→One occurrence is an incident. Two occurrences is a coincidence. Three is a pattern
    17→worth systematizing.
    18→
    19→## Where to Look
    20→
    21→On each heartbeat, scan:
    22→- `learnings/failures/` — failure classes, not just task names
    23→- `learnings/corrections/` — human corrections often reveal systematic gaps
    24→- `memory/daily/builder/` — Builder's recent struggles
    25→- `skills/proposed/` — check whether proposed skills address a common theme
    26→
    27→Look for the underlying class, not the surface symptom — multiple incidents may be one pattern.
    28→
    29→## Writing a Pattern Skill
    30→
    31→When a pattern is confirmed, write a skill to `skills/proposed/{scope}-{name}/SKILL.md`
    32→that:
    33→- Names the pattern clearly in the description
    34→- States when the pattern applies (not too broad, not too narrow)

{"type": "user", "message": {"role": "user", "content": [{"tool_use_id": "toolu_01HqQ7cPoBMJGY7Zx5gdZBvm", "type": "tool_result", "content": "     1→---
     2→name: domain-interview
     3→description: How to interview the human to extract what they actually want. What questions to ask and when to stop asking.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## When to Interview
     7→
     8→Before writing an NLSpec for anything non-trivial, interview the human if:
     9→- The intent is ambiguous about key behaviors (not just implementation details)
    10→- The scope could be interpreted narrowly or broadly with materially different outcomes
    11→- The domain is unfamiliar and you lack enough context to make reasonable choices
    12→
    13→Do NOT interview about implementation details — make a reasonable choice and flag it in the spec's constraints section.
    14→
    15→## What to Ask
    16→
    17→**Scope boundaries** — what is explicitly in and out of scope
    18→- "Should this handle X, or is that a future concern?"
    19→**Success criteria** — what does "done" look like for the human
    20→- "How would you know this is working correctly?"
    21→- "What would make this version a success even if Z isn't done?"
    22→
    23→**Constraints** — real constraints vs. preferences
    24→- "Is [technology choice] required, or do you have flexibility?"
    25→- "Are there existing systems this must integrate with?"
    26→
    27→## When to Stop Asking
    28→
    29→Stop when you can answer this question confidently:
    30→> "If I write a spec now and Builder implements it, would the human be satisfied?"
    31→
    32→Indicators you can stop:

     4→---
     5→
     6→## Daily Log Maintenance
     7→
     8→Daily logs in `memory/daily/{agent}/` are append-only during the day. Librarian's
     9→job is to prevent them from becoming a graveyard of stale entries.
    10→
    11→On each heartbeat:
    12→- Scan daily logs older than 7 days
    13→- If a daily log contains findings that have not been promoted to long-term memory,
    14→  promote them now
    15→- If a daily log contains only routine heartbeat notes with no durable learning,
    16→  it can be archived (move to `memory/daily/{agent}/archive/`)
    17→- Never delete daily logs — always archive
    18→
    19→## Long-term Memory Promotion
    20→
    21→`memory/{agent}/MEMORY.md` is the agent's curated, durable memory. Promote to it:
    22→- Conventions established for the factory or a project
    23→- Decisions made and their rationale
    24→- Patterns confirmed across multiple observations
    25→- Key constraints (e.g., "agents.yaml is human-only, never modify it")
    26→
    27→Do NOT promote:
    28→- Task-specific details that won't apply to future tasks
    29→- Observations that are already in `memory/shared/KNOWLEDGE.md`
    30→
    31→## Contradiction Detection
    32→
    33→When reviewing `memory/shared/KNOWLEDGE.md` or individual agent memories, watch for:
    34→- Two entries that describe the same convention differently
    35→- A newer entry that supersedes an older one without noting the change\
